title: Listening - Part 1
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.
instructions: You will hear three different extracts. For questions 1 – 6, choose the answer (A, B or C) which fits best according to what you hear. There are two questions for each extract.

extracts.context: You hear two friends, a man and a woman, discussing a new language-learning app.
extracts.audio_script: Woman: So, how’s the Spanish coming along with that new app? Man: It’s okay for building up vocabulary, I suppose. But [1]I’m finding the speech recognition software incredibly frustrating[/1]. It keeps marking me wrong even when I’m positive my pronunciation is spot on. Woman: I had the same issue. I think it struggles with any kind of regional inflection. Man: Exactly! It’s making me paranoid about my accent. Woman: [2]The real draw for me, though, is the gamification side of it[/2]. I actually find myself looking forward to my daily streak. Man: See, that’s where we differ. I find the constant reminders and 'leagues' a bit gimmicky. I’d rather just have more complex grammar explanations. Woman: Well, different strokes for different folks, I guess.

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: So, how’s the Spanish coming along with that new app?

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: It’s okay for building up vocabulary, I suppose. But I’m finding the speech recognition software incredibly frustrating. It keeps marking me wrong even when I’m positive my pronunciation is spot on.

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: I had the same issue. I think it struggles with any kind of regional inflection.

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: Exactly! It’s making me paranoid about my accent.

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: The real draw for me, though, is the gamification side of it. I actually find myself looking forward to my daily streak.

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: See, that’s where we differ. I find the constant reminders and 'leagues' a bit gimmicky. I’d rather just have more complex grammar explanations.

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: Well, different strokes for different folks, I guess.

questions.question: What is the man's main complaint about the app?

options.A: It doesn't provide enough new words.
options.B: The audio feedback is technically unreliable.
options.C: It focuses too much on regional dialects.

questions.explanation: He mentions the speech recognition software is 'incredibly frustrating' because it marks him wrong despite correct pronunciation.

questions.question: What does the woman identify as the app's most appealing feature?

options.C: The elements designed to encourage regular use.

questions.explanation: She mentions 'gamification' and 'daily streaks' as the 'real draw', referring to features that motivate daily practice.

extracts.context: You hear two professionals discussing the rise of 'ghost kitchens' (restaurants with no physical dining space).
extracts.audio_script: Man: It’s fascinating how many of these delivery-only brands are popping up lately. Woman: It makes perfect economic sense. Without the overheads of a prime high-street location, they can pivot their menu in days. Man: [3]But don't you think something is lost when the connection between the chef and the customer is entirely digital?[/3] Woman: Perhaps, but in today's market, convenience is king. People care more about the speed of delivery than the 'soul' of the kitchen. Man: I'm not so sure. [4]I suspect we'll see a backlash once the novelty wears off and people start craving a tangible experience again[/4]. Woman: Maybe for high-end dining, but for a Tuesday night takeaway? I doubt it. Man: Still, it’s a bit bleak for the hospitality industry as a whole, isn't it? Everything becoming so transactional. Woman: It’s just evolution, Sonia. Adapt or die.

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: It’s fascinating how many of these delivery-only brands are popping up lately.

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: It makes perfect economic sense. Without the overheads of a prime high-street location, they can pivot their menu in days.

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: But don't you think something is lost when the connection between the chef and the customer is entirely digital?

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: Perhaps, but in today's market, convenience is king. People care more about the speed of delivery than the 'soul' of the kitchen.

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: I'm not so sure. I suspect we'll see a backlash once the novelty wears off and people start craving a tangible experience again.

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: Maybe for high-end dining, but for a Tuesday night takeaway? I doubt it.

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: Still, it’s a bit bleak for the hospitality industry as a whole, isn't it? Everything becoming so transactional.

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: It’s just evolution, Sonia. Adapt or die.

questions.question: The man expresses concern about...

options.A: the hygiene standards in delivery-only kitchens.
options.B: the lack of personal interaction in the food industry.
options.C: the rising cost of digital marketing for restaurants.

questions.explanation: He asks if 'something is lost' when the connection is 'entirely digital', implying a concern for the loss of human/personal connection.

questions.question: What is the man's prediction for the future of this trend?

options.A: It will eventually lead to a return to traditional dining values.
options.B: It will completely replace physical restaurants within a decade.
options.C: It will only be successful in suburban areas.

questions.explanation: He suggests there will be a 'backlash' and that people will start 'craving a tangible experience again'.

extracts.context: You hear two young people in Ireland, Connor and Emily, talking about a 'slow travel' trip across Europe.
extracts.audio_script: Man: So, you’re finally back from your three-week train odyssey! How was it? Woman: Honestly, Connor, it was transformative. [5]The sense of scale you get when you actually watch the landscape change is something a flight can never give you[/5]. Man: I bet. Though I’d imagine the sheer length of the journeys got a bit tedious after a while? Woman: Surprisingly, no. I used the time to catch up on all the reading I’ve been putting off. It felt like a gift of time, rather than a waste of it. Man: [6]I’m still not convinced about the price tag, though. Last time I checked, a cross-border rail pass was nearly double the cost of a budget flight[/6]. Woman: It is an investment, I’ll give you that. But when you factor in the lack of airport stress and the environmental aspect, it’s a no-brainer for me. Man: I suppose. Maybe I’ll consider it for my next summer break.

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: So, you’re finally back from your three-week train odyssey! How was it?

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: Honestly, Connor, it was transformative. The sense of scale you get when you actually watch the landscape change is something a flight can never give you.

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: I bet. Though I’d imagine the sheer length of the journeys got a bit tedious after a while?

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: Surprisingly, no. I used the time to catch up on all the reading I’ve been putting off. It felt like a gift of time, rather than a waste of it.

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: I’m still not convinced about the price tag, though. Last time I checked, a cross-border rail pass was nearly double the cost of a budget flight.

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: It is an investment, I’ll give you that. But when you factor in the lack of airport stress and the environmental aspect, it’s a no-brainer for me.

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: I suppose. Maybe I’ll consider it for my next summer break.

questions.question: What did the woman appreciate most about travelling by train?

options.A: The opportunity to meet people from different cultures.
options.C: The superior comfort compared to aeroplanes.

questions.explanation: She mentions the 'sense of scale' and 'watching the landscape change', which relates to geographical perspective.

questions.question: What is the man's main reservation about 'slow travel'?

options.A: The financial cost of the tickets.
options.B: The unreliability of international train schedules.
options.C: The potential boredom on long journeys.

questions.explanation: He explicitly mentions the 'price tag' and that it is 'double the cost' of flying.
